12+ years shaping digital products that drive growth, clarity, and business outcomes for global brands and startups.

I solve complex challenges with elegant, data-driven solutions that have scaled to millions of users and generated tens of millions in revenue. Embracing the new AI era, I integrate generative intelligence and automation to craft tomorrow's products today.

Started as a graphic designer 20 years ago, then moved by a passion for creating experiences, became a product designer in 2013. I've shaped digital experiences across Brazil, Europe, and the US. I love breaking down complex systems: from high-fidelity prototypes to user-centered solutions.

I believe in the power of collaboration and enjoy working closely with cross-functional teams to bring ideas to life. My goal is to create products that not only meet user and business needs but make a significant change in this world.
Outside of design, I enjoy exploring new technologies, following aviation, and indulging my passion for cars. My dog keeps me company during workdays, adding joy to my daily routine.

Redesigned the operational architecture of a Brazilian healthcare company — replacing ~$50K/yr in fragmented third-party systems with Argos, an AI-assisted operations platform built for biomedical maintenance teams. Expanded service capacity from 210 to 333 active clients without growing the team.
The Real Problem Wasn't the Website

Okamed was paying ~$50K/yr for fragmented third-party platforms that still didn't support how their teams actually worked. Technicians were stuck on desktop tools in environments that demanded mobile. Managers had no real-time visibility into field operations. Leadership had no reliable reporting on SLAs, productivity, or service capacity. The engagement started as a website redesign — but within the first weeks of field research, it became clear the actual problem was the company's operational architecture itself.

With limited structured data available, I ran field research to understand how technicians, managers, and operations teams actually worked — not how the client assumed they did. What surfaced was a layer of hidden inefficiencies the company had never fully mapped: desktop-dependent workflows, fragmented communication, manual processes, and near-zero visibility into field performance. That research changed everything. The brief was no longer about redesigning a website — it was about replacing the entire operational layer.

Working alongside a single developer and using AI as a methodology throughout — accelerating design, iteration, documentation, prototyping, and frontend implementation — a two-person team delivered what traditionally requires a full squad. Argos replaced every third-party tool with a unified platform: mobile-first workflows with offline access for technicians in hospitals with unstable connectivity, service order management, equipment tracking, real-time operational dashboards, and SLA reporting tailored to Okamed's model.

Okamed expanded service capacity from 210 to 333 active clients without growing the team. The company now operates across 10 Brazilian states and has started expanding into Latin America. The result reframed what product design can mean in an enterprise context: not creating screens, but redesigning how a business operates, scales, and delivers value.

No dedicated team, no roadmap — and a product running on an abandoned Salesforce platform. Led research, uncovered high-impact quick-wins aligned with Harmony's design language, defined a 6-month roadmap, and shipped a fully rebuilt experience across navigation, product education, and mobile — cutting mobile drop-off from ~90% to under 10%.

The Online Store had no dedicated team when the opportunity emerged. The product ran on an abandoned Salesforce platform — technically constrained, without behavioral instrumentation, and visually disconnected from the rest of Realtor.com. I worked with the design manager and the senior manager to define what the actual problem was before proposing any solution.

We ran heuristic analysis and structured user interviews. The research surfaced a concentration of quick-win pain points — and I deliberately prioritized fixes that also moved the platform closer to Harmony, Realtor.com's design system. Short-term improvements doubled as groundwork for the full migration. To measure impact from day one, we integrated Knotch for heatmaps and engagement tracking, Amplitude for event-level drop-off and filter usage analysis, and session replay tools to surface exact points of confusion in the flow.

The rebuild ran on Salesforce LWC — chosen for its reusable, composable architecture and tight integration with Salesforce CMS and Apex for real-time product availability and pricing. Navigation was restructured around logical product hierarchy with live filters that update without page reloads and persistent breadcrumbs to keep users oriented. Product pages gained expanded technical specs, compatibility data, and side-by-side comparison tools. Mobile had been catastrophic at ~90% drop-off — every element was redesigned as touch-first, and load times were optimized for mobile networks.
A Product — and a Team — Born
Mobile drop-off cut from approximately 90% to under 10%. The work justified investment: Realtor.com expanded the team, hired to grow the product, and I was promoted to lead design on UpNest.

After the Online Store work led to team expansion, I was promoted to lead design on UpNest — another Realtor.com product that needed a full UX revision. UpNest is the B2B CRM used by real estate agents to manage leads, track their sales funnel, and onboard into Realtor.com's agent ecosystem.

Overhauled the CRM structure, hierarchy, and interaction logic across platforms. Redesigned agent onboarding and lead management flows. Surfaced contextual actions where agents needed them and improved lead segmentation — so agents could prioritize the right opportunities and track the funnel with clarity.

Led UX design for the Rentals experience on one of the largest real estate platforms in the US — 190M+ monthly users. Shipped rental search improvements, contributed to the Harmony design system implementation for realtor.com/rentals, and co-built an AI-powered conversational search MVP.

After two years shaping Avail.co — a landlord property management platform later acquired by Move Corporation, Realtor.com's parent company — I joined Realtor.com directly to lead design on the Rentals experience. That background in rental product gave me context few designers brought to this team.

Redesigned the rental search and filtering experience end-to-end, improving lead quality and submission rates. Contributed to the rollout of Harmony — Realtor.com's design system (formerly Haven) — on the Rentals product. The work involved cataloging cross-platform divergences across web, iOS, and Android, consolidating one-off patterns into single instances with variants, and assembling standardized components back into the product's master files. A platform-wide overhaul that shaped much of what's visible at realtor.com/rentals today.
Co-built an AI-powered conversational search MVP with engineering — designed around relevance and user intent, focusing on how users actually describe what they're looking for in a rental home.

FanFest had no established design system, UX direction, or product language when I joined. The challenge was to go from zero to a platform credible enough to sign PSG, ManCity, NBA, and Sky Sports — and to do that while the product was still being validated.

Established the full design system and UX/UI direction from scratch. Designed gamification, AI personalization, loyalty systems, and real-time fan experience flows. Worked directly with the CEO on product vision, investor decks, and business design strategy — from MVP through fundraising.
30M+ remote fans engaged on the platform. $3M+ in customer revenue. $5M+ in funding secured. Up to 30% paid conversion in early pilots.

Enterprise Power BI dashboards across PepsiCo's global teams had inconsistent KPI visualization patterns, causing interpretation friction and low adoption. Decision-makers across regions were reading the same data differently — slowing reporting cycles and generating avoidable support tickets.

Embedded into PepsiCo's cross-functional environment — working alongside product, data engineering, and global operations teams across regions. Standardized layout patterns, interaction logic, and data storytelling principles. Built foundational design guardrails to ensure consistency across internal analytics tools, regardless of team or reporting domain.

Working in a highly matrixed enterprise means design decisions require broader alignment than in a typical product team. The tradeoff was speed vs. consistency: moving faster would mean local solutions that couldn't scale. Choosing to invest in shared guardrails upfront took longer but produced something every team could build on.
Dashboard adoption up 23%. Support tickets down 41%. Design guardrails adopted across teams as the standard for enterprise analytics UX — reducing friction between data and decision-makers at scale.

Avail.co's rental storefront pages had fragmented, inconsistent structure that was depressing conversion and requiring engineering involvement for every content update. Onboarding and payment flows had friction points driving landlord churn across a platform serving 700K+ customers.

Redesigned all storefront pages from scratch — simplified structure, unified components into a reusable library. Built a lead-generation feature connecting renters interested in buying their first home. Improved onboarding and leasing/payment workflows to reduce friction for both landlords and tenants.
70% increase in user conversion. Marketing team now updates pages 84% faster without engineering dependencies. Lead-gen solution generating 10K+ qualified leads per month across 5M active users.

Avail was acquired by Move Corporation — Realtor.com's parent company — and became a sub-product of the Realtor.com ecosystem. Realtor.com rental listings started surfacing within Avail's landlord experience, and Avail's property management tools were recommended to landlords coming from Realtor.com. The rental domain expertise I built here became the foundation for my work on Realtor.com/Rentals.

The Online Store — the B2B product enabling real estate agents to purchase leads and manage listings — ran on an abandoned Salesforce platform. Mobile drop-off was approximately 90%, adoption was critically low, there was no dedicated team, and the product was visually and technically disconnected from the rest of Realtor.com's ecosystem.

Worked alongside the design manager (who brought the initial proposal) and a senior manager to scope the problem before any design started. Ran heuristic analysis and structured user interviews. Research surfaced quick-win pain points for the first phase — and I deliberately prioritized fixes that also aligned the platform's components and patterns with Haven, Realtor.com's design system. The goal: short-term improvements that doubled as migration groundwork, so the eventual full transition away from Salesforce would be significantly less disruptive. Together we defined a 6-month roadmap. Led design from findings through delivery.

There was no pre-existing team or budget — the work had to justify its own investment. That meant ruthless prioritization: fix the high-pain, low-effort issues first to demonstrate value, while making sure those fixes also moved the product closer to Haven's design language. Not every improvement was purely about UX — some decisions were architectural bets on the migration that was coming. Some improvements were intentionally deferred to give the new team their own problems to solve.

Mobile drop-off cut from approximately 90% to under 10%. The roadmap justified investment — Realtor.com expanded the team, hired to grow the product. I was promoted to lead design on UpNest as a direct result.

UpNest had structural and hierarchy issues that slowed agents' daily workflows across the full funnel. Agent onboarding was fragmented, lead segmentation was unclear, and contextual actions were buried — making it hard for agents to manage their pipeline and understand where they stood in the sales funnel.

Promoted from the Online Store work to lead UpNest's UX revision. Redesigned agent onboarding and lead management flows. Overhauled CRM structure, hierarchy, and interaction logic across platforms. Surfaced contextual actions at the right moments and restructured lead segmentation for clarity.

Agents completing tasks 40–60% faster across the funnel. Improved agent satisfaction and NPS. Better lead tracking and pipeline visibility. More coherent onboarding path for agents entering the Realtor.com ecosystem.

UpNest was a product with a lot of accumulated debt — structural decisions that made sense individually but didn't add up to a coherent experience. What I'd do differently: push harder for a unified information architecture review before touching individual flows. The onboarding work surfaced upstream issues that slowed us down mid-project.

Rental lead submissions were flat despite 190M+ monthly users. The search experience had friction — filters were incomplete, the path from search to qualified lead was unclear, and the Rentals product needed to align with Harmony, Realtor.com's platform-wide design system overhaul.

Redesigned the rental search and filtering experience end-to-end. Contributed to the Harmony design system implementation on the Rentals product. Co-built an AI-powered conversational search MVP with engineering — focused on relevance and user intent.
+12.5% unique lead submissions. +7% more qualified leads from improved search and filters. AI-powered conversational search MVP shipped internally. Realtor.com/rentals aligned with Harmony.

Okamed was spending ~$50K/yr on fragmented third-party platforms that still didn't support how their teams actually worked. Technicians were locked to desktop tools in hospital environments that demanded mobile. Managers had no real-time visibility into field operations. Leadership had no reliable SLA or productivity reporting. The engagement started as a website redesign — but field research revealed the real problem was the entire operational architecture.

Ran field research with technicians, managers, and ops teams to map how work actually happened — not how leadership assumed it did. What surfaced was a layer of hidden inefficiencies nobody had fully mapped: desktop-dependent workflows, fragmented communication, manual processes, near-zero field visibility. Redefined the brief entirely. Working alongside a single developer and using AI as a methodology throughout — accelerating design, prototyping, documentation, and frontend implementation — built Argos in 3 months. The platform replaced every third-party tool: mobile-first workflows with offline access for hospitals with unstable connectivity, service order management, equipment tracking, real-time dashboards, and SLA reporting.

The original scope was a website redesign — a fraction of what was actually built. Redefining the brief mid-engagement meant pushing back on the client's initial framing and earning trust before expanding scope. The decision to use AI as a core methodology (not a finishing tool) was a deliberate bet: it compressed a 6-month project into 3, but required a developer willing to pair closely on implementation rather than receive traditional handoffs.

Okamed expanded from 210 to 333 active clients without growing the team. Now operating across 10 Brazilian states with LATAM expansion underway. ~$50K/yr in fragmented tools replaced by a single platform. Two people, three months, full delivery.
